Facts.text: Trojan War: Troy is sacked and burned according to calculations by Eratosthenes

Emperor Taizong of Tang sends envoys to the Xueyantuo bearing gold and silk for the release of Chinese prisoners captured on the northern frontier during the transition from the Sui to Tang dynasty and succeeds in freeing 80,000
Smoke Monkey ascends to the throne of the Maya city of Copán, after the capture and killing of his predecessor 18 Rabbit by rival city Quirigua
Diplomatic dispute at Tang Chinese capital Chang'an when Abbasid Arabs and Uyghur Turks both arrive to offer tribute. Settlement reached when both enter at same time through two different gates.
Basilica of St Denis is dedicated near Paris, the first fully Gothic church
Harderwijk (Netherlands) becomes a city
Alexios Apokaukos, chief minister of the Byzantine Empire, is lynched by political prisoners
Hundred Years' War: the start of the Battle of Jargeau (France)
Battle of Sauchieburn, rebellion against the Scottish crown results in death of King James III
British admiral Sir Thomas Pert reaches Hudson Bay
Spanish co-land guardian Duke of Medinaceli arrives in Lock
England grants Sir Humphrey Gilbert a patent to explore and colonize North America
Florentine scientist Evangelista Torricelli describes his invention of the mercury barometer in 1643 in a letter to Michelangelo Ricci
Four Days Battle - naval battle in the southern North Seas during Second Anglo-Dutch War: Dutch victory with ten English ships destroyed [OS 1-4 June]
Cardinal Fleury succeeds Duke of Bourbon as French premier
Jews of Algiers escape attacking Spanish Army
Construction of the first British ship on the Pacific coast begins at Nootka Sound, British Columbia
Russian explorer Gerasim Izmailov reaches Alaska
1st American stove patent is granted to Robert Haeterick
Spanish Mission San José founded by Franciscan Padre Fermin Francisco de Lasuen at the Ohlone Village of Oroysom in Alta California (now Freemont, California)
Gas Light Co. of Baltimore founded
The first cornerstone is laid for Fort Hamilton in New York City
The Broad Street Riot occurs in Boston, fueled by ethnic tensions between English-Americans and Irish-Americans
Battle of Laurel Hill, West Virginia - Confederate General Robert S. Garnett is the first general killed during the Civil War
300 feet (90 m) of Meiggs Wharf in San Francisco is washed away in a storm
Battle at Cynthiana Kentucky: General Hobsons unit caught
Battle of Trevilian Station, the bloodiest and largest all-cavalry battle of the American Civil War, begins in Louisa County, Virginia
CSS Alabama, a Confederate screw sloop-of-war, arrives in port at Cherbourg, France
The Agra High Court (now the Allahabad High Court) is established in India
First stone Amstel Brewery opens in Amsterdam
DC is given a new government by Congress, 3 commissioners appointed by president (change in 1974)
Puerto Rican flag adopted
The Limelight Department, one of the world's first film studios, is officially established in Melbourne, Australia
Charles Duryea patents a gas-driven automobile
US Assay Office in Deadwood South Dakota authorized
Emperor De Zong proclaims reforms in Peking
-12] Battle at Diamond Hill: British troops chase General Botha
Cook Islands annexed and proclaimed part of New Zealand
King Alexander I and his wife Queen Draga Mašin of Serbia are assassinated in the royal palace in Belgrade by a group of army officers led by Captain Dragutin Dimitrijević, as part of a military coup
Chicago Cubs pitcher Bob Wicker no-hits NY Giants, wins in 12th on 1 hitter, 1-0
German Lt. General Lothar von Trotha lands in Swapokmund, German South West Africa
Pennsylvania Railroad debuts the fastest train in the world, capable of traveling from NY to Chicago in 18 hours
George Dennett, aided by Gilbert Jessop, dismisses Northamptonshire for 12 runs, the lowest total in first-class cricket
Northants all out for 12 v Gloucestershire
The Greek national assembly adopts a more liberal constitution
Grand Vizir Mahmud Shevket Pasha is assassinated, resulting in continuing Young Turk terrorism until WWI
Brazil adopts women's suffrage
Bene Brak Palestine founded
Koos Vorrink elected President of Arbeiders Jeugd Organisatie (AJC) Socialist Youth Movement
Disarmament conference in Geneva fails
Inventor Edwin Armstrong gives the first public demonstration of FM broadcasting in the United States, at Alpine, New Jersey
International Surrealist Exhibition opens in London, England
Presbyterian Church of America founded in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Marx Brothers' "A Day At The Races", directed by Sam Wood, is released in the US
5.0 Earthquake in Belgium kills 2, strongest in 45 years
Cincinnati Red Johnny Vander Meer no-hits Boston Braves, 3-0
Compton scores 1st Test Cricket ton (102 v Aust) aged 20 yrs 19 days
England cricket team declare at 8 for 658 v Australia at Trent Bridge
British PremierWinston Churchillflies to Orleans
Italy declares war on Allies
World War II: British forces bomb Genoa and Turin in Italy
World War II: First attack of the Italian Air force on the island of Malta
2nd great raid on Jews of Amsterdam
Vichy-French planes bomb Tel Aviv, killing 20 Jews
US and USSR sign Lend-Lease agreement during WW II
WWII: British invades Pantelleria, a tiny island south of Sicily
15 US aircraft carriers attack Japanese bases on Marianas
The first Serbian Orthodox cathedral in the US, the Cathedral of St. Sava, opens in NYC
WW II sugar rationing finally ends in US (began May 28, 1942)
Emile Zatopek runs world record 10,000m (29:28.2)
Mozambique becomes an oversea province of Portugal
NY Times reports the NYC subway will auction off unclaimed items left behind on the trains
"Amos 'n Andy" TV Comedy, also radio from '29; last aired on CBS
Test Cricket debut of Alan Davidson at Trent Bridge
1st magnesium jet airplane flies
12 die in a train crash in Vroman, Colorado
UN Security council sends observers to Lebanon
Postmaster General bans D. H. Lawrence's book 'Lady Chatterley's Lover' as obscene; overruled by US Court of Appeals in March of 1960
Saunders-Roe SR.N1, the first practical hovercraft, performs its first public flight
House packed with wedding celebrants collapses killing 30 (Pakistan)
"Winston Churchill" last airs on ABC-TV
Norm Cash becomes 1st Detroit Tiger to hit a ball out of Tiger Stadium
Brothers John and Clarence Anglin and fellow inmate Frank Morris escape from Alcatraz Island prison, the only ones to do so
Gov Wallace tries to prevent blacks registering at University of Alabama
Greek government of Karamanlis resigns
US PresidentJohn F. Kennedysays segregation is morally wrong and that it is "time to act"
Chicago police break up Rolling Stones press conference on a traffic island in the middle of Michigan Avenue
Manfred Mann record Do Wah Diddy Diddy Dum Diddy Do
West Germany seeks talks with Czechoslovakia
"(I'm A) Road Runner" by Jr Walker & The All-Stars peaks at #20
"I Am A Rock" by Simon & Garfunkel peaks at #3
"Sloop John B" by The Beach Boys hits #1 in UK
French and German media mistakenly report death of Roger Daltry
Chicago Cubs (7) & NY Mets (4) tie record of 11 HRs in a game
Mexico becomes a member of the Berne Convention copyright treaty
Race riot in Tampa, Florida; National Guard mobilizes
US leaves Wheelus AFB Libya
US & Japan sign accord to return Okinawa to Japan
US ends ban on China trade
31°F lowest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland, Ohio in June
Gun battle between Loyalist and Republican paramilitaries break out in the Oldpark area of Belfast
KPAT-AM in Berkeley, California returns to original call letters KRE, marking 50 years since first on-air broadcxast
Libya nationalizes Bunker Hunt concession; Nigeria acquires 35 percent participation in Shell-BP concession
Bundy victim Georgann Hawkins disappears from the University of Washington in Seattle
1st oil pumped from North Sea oilfield
Greece adopts constitution
One-day international cricket debut ofJaved Miandadat age 17 years and 364 days
"Magnificent Marble Machine" last airs on NBC-TV
Anti-apartheid advocate Dumisa Ntsebeza arrested in South Africa
Beatles "Rock & Roll Music" compilation LP is released in USA
"I'm Your Boogie Man" by KC and the Sunshine Band peaks at #1
Dutch marines rescued hostages from a Moluccan held train in Holland
Main Street Electrical Parade premieres in the Magic Kingdom at Walt Disney World
Christa Tybus of London sets record of 24½ hrs of hula-hoop
Joseph Freeman Jr is 1st black priest in Church Of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints
K-Ingleside streetcar converts to METRO service (San Francisco)
Cannibal Issei Sagawa kills Dutch student
Mariners beat Orioles 8-2 at Kingdome, then players go on strike
Richter Scale 6.9 magnitude Golbaf earthquake in Iran, kills at least 2,000
Israel and Syria stop fighting in Lebanon
"Always Something There To Remind Me" by Naked Eyes peaks at #8
"Cool Places" by Sparks & Jane Wiedlin peaks at #49
"Faithfully" by Journey peaks at #12
"Theme From Doctor Detroit" by Devo peaks at #59
"When I'm With You" by Sheriff peaks at #61
Zhu Jianhua of China set a new world record in high jump at 7-9 14
Funeral for S Nakagawa & burial of half of his ashes
Michael Larson shown winning record $110,237 on American TV game show "Press Your Luck" by memorizing patterns
US Supreme Court declares illegally obtained evidence may be admitted at trial if it could be proved that it would have been discovered legally
Russian space probe Vega 1 lands on Venus
Von Hayes becomes 21st to hit 2 HRs in an inning (Phils-26, Mets-7)
WJW-AM/TV in Cleveland Ohio change call letters to WRMR
"Blacke's Magic" last airs on NBC-TV
"Everything Your Heart Desires" by Daryl Hall and John Oates peaks at #3
"Rooty Toot Toot" by John Cougar Mellencamp peaks at #61
Galina Chistyakova of USSR sets long jump woman's record (24"8½")
NY Yankees Rick Rhoden becomes first pitcher since AL's adoption of the rule in 1973 to DH (0 for 1 with an RBI) in an 8–6 victory over Baltimore Orioles
Federal judge sentenced former national security adviser John M Poindexter to 6 months for making false statements to Congress
Supreme Court says law prohibiting desecration of US flag unconstitutional
Microsoft releases MS DOS 5.0
Owners approve sale of Seattle Mariners to a Japanese group
US Olympic baseball team plays 1st exhibition game, beat Venezuela 20-0
"Jurassic Park," directed bySteven Spielbergand starring Sam Neill and Jeff Goldblum, opens and sets a box office weekend record of $502 million
"Scattergories" game show last airs on NBC-TV
"Scrabble," second run," TV Game Show; last airs on NBC-TV
"Meet The Flintstones" by The B-52's (stylized for this release as The B.C. 52's) from the 1994 film The Flintstones peaks at #33
Cbox is 39'115 long, 20'85 wide, & 8' high in Jacksonville, Florida - largest popcorn container is 6,619.76 cubic feet full of popped corn
Drunken police officer shoots 7 people dead in Falun, Sweden
Moshood Abiola becomes President of Nigeria
Rondell White has six hits and hits for the cycle in 13 innings
Exxon states that it will begin work on its $15-billion Sakhalin I oil and natural gas development in Russia's Far East
Compaq Computer pays $9.6 billion for Digital Equipment Corporation; largest high-tech acquisition at the time

Saudi Arabia seizes ownership, effective June 7, of the 1.6-million-barrels-per-day IPSA pipeline that had carried Iraqi crude oil to the Saudi Red Sea port of Mu'jiz prior to Iraq's invasion of Kuwait
Antonio Meucci is acknowledged as the first inventor of the telephone by the United States Congress
Cassini-Huygens makes its closest flyby of Phoebe
60th Tony Awards: "Jersey Boys" (musical) & "The History Boys" (play) win
Bulgarian authorities declare the country is officially in recession after dropping 1.6% in the last quarter of 2008

Texas mother hit by lightning while standing in her kitchen in her Texas home. Witnesses say lightning came through a light fixture, struck her chest and exited her foot. Survived after three days in hospital.
Philip IIrecognizes the rights and privileges of local nobles and chieftains in the Philippines, creating a ruling class of native nobility known as the Principalía
Duke of MonmouthJames Scottrebels against Catholic KingJames II
Benjamin Franklininvents his Franklin stove
Republicans pickRutherford B. Hayesas US presidential candidate
David Beattyand 150 men from HMS Barfleur land as part of a force of 2,400 defending Tientsin from 15,000 Chinese troops plus Boxers
Universal Negro Improvement Association founded byMarcus Garvey
King Alexander assumes the throne of Greece after his fatherConstantine Iabdicates under pressure by allied armies occupying Athens
Republicans nominateWarren G. Hardingfor US president
Charles Lindberghis awarded the 1st Distinguished Flying Cross
Future Baseball Hall of Fame sluggerBabe Ruthhits 19th & 20th homers of MLB season record 60 HRs in New York's 6-4 win over Cleveland Indians at Yankees Stadium
Alfred Hitchcock's silent film "Case Of Jonathan Drew" (or "The Lodger") is released
British ruler KingGeorge VIand Queen Elizabeth taste their first "hot dogs" at a party atFDR's Hyde Park residence during their US visit
Perennial Christmas film "Miracle on 34th St" starring John Payne andMaureen O'Hara, directed by George Seaton is released
New York Yankees outfielderRoger Marishits 19th & 20th of 61 HRs
"Julie & Carol at Carnegie Hall", musical comedy/variety television special withJulie Andrews&Carol Burnettpremieres on CBS
US PresidentJohn F. Kennedyaccepts an honorary degree from Yale
"Paint It Black" byThe Rolling Stonespeaks at #1 in the US
"The Ballad OfJohn & Yoko" by The Beatles hits #1 in UK; recordinginvolved only John(guitars, vocals, percussion) and Paul (bass, drums, piano, backing voice)
Mel Stottlemyresets an American League record by making his 272nd consecutive start without pitching a game in relief
"Nashville", directed byRobert Altmanand starring Ned Beatty and Ronee Blakley, is released
Australian bandAC/DCbegin their 1st headline tour of Britain
Chuck Berrypleads guilty to income tax evasion, sentenced to 4 months
"E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial", directed bySteven Spielberg, starring Henry Thomas andDrew Barrymore, is released
Defending championLarry HolmesTKOs Gerry Cooney in 13 for WBC and The Ring heavyweight boxing titles at Caesars Palace, LV, Nevada
PopeJohn Paul IIvisits Argentina
"My Love" byLionel Richiepeaks at #5
George Gallowayis elected as the MP for Glasgow Hillhead in the general election
Margaret Thatcheris the first British Prime Minister in 160 years to win a third consecutive term
"We All Sleep Alone" byCherpeaks at #14
MLB Texas RangersNolan Ryanpitches his 6th no-hitter beating Oakland, in the 9th inning, he retires Ken Phelps,Rickey HendersonandWillie Randolph
UN appoints British-Australian singerOlivia Newton-Johnas environmental ambassador
Natalie Colereleases "Unforgettable...with Love", covering her father's standards including a "virtual duet" of title track; wins 6 Grammy Awards, including Album of the Year, 1992
Tracy Austin, 29, is youngest inductee of International Tennis Hall of Fame
Bob Dole, (Sen-R-KS), resigns from US senate to run for president
"American Idol" created by Simon Fuller with judgesSimon Cowell,Paula Abdul, and Randy Jackson premieres on Fox-TV
Ronald Reagan's funeral is held at Washington National Cathedral

Canadian Prime MinisterStephen Harpermade an official historic apology to Canada's First Nations in regard to a residential school abuse in which children were isolated from their homes, families and cultures for a century
Continental Congress creates committee to draft a Declaration of Independence withThomas Jefferson, John Adams, Benjamin Franklin, Roger Sherman, and Robert R. Livingston as members
1st US Marines (600) land at Guantanamo, Cuba during the Spanish–American War
Opening batsman Len Hutton becomes the 1st professional cricketer to captain England in Tests
Buddhist monk Thích Quảng Đức immolates himself at a Saigon intersection, creating one of the Vietnam War's most iconic images
The World Health Organization declares H1N1 swine flu to be a global pandemic, the first such incident in over forty years
John IV, Duke of Brabant and Limburg (founded Leuven University), born in Arras, modern-day France (d. 1427)
Edmund Tudor, 1st Earl of Richmond and patriarch of the Tudor Dynasty, born in Much Hadham Palace, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1456)
Anne Neville, English queen, wife of Richard III of England, born in Warwick Castle, Warwickshire (d. 1485)
Barnabe Googe, English poet, born in Alvingham, Lincolnshire (d. 1594)
George Wither, English writer (Abuses Stript and Whipt), born in Hampshire, England (d. 1667)
Francesco Antonio Bonporti, Italian priest and amateur composer, born in Padua, Republic of Venice (d. 1749)
Francis Edward James Keith, Scottish soldier and Prussian field marshal, born in Inverugie Castle, Scotland (d. 1758)
Francesco Antonio Vallotti, Italian organist, composer and theorist, born in Vercelli, Duchy of Savoy, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1780)
José António Carlos de Seixas, Portuguese composer, born in Coimbra, Portugal (d. 1742)
Edward Capell, English critic, born in Troston Hall, Suffolk, England (d. 1781)
François-Charles de Velbrück, German ecclesiastic and Prince-Bishop of Liege (1772-84), born in Garath Castle near Düsseldorf (d. 1784)
Johann Georg Palitzsch, German astronomer, 1st saw Halley's comet on return, born in Prohlis, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (now Germany) (d. 1788)
Luigi Gatti, Italian tenor, kapellmeister, priest, and composer, born in Lazise, Verona, Republic of Venice (d. 1817)
Joseph Warren, American physician and soldier, born in Roxbury, Province of Massachusetts Bay (d. 1775)
John Constable, English landscape painter (Hay Wain), born in East Bergholt, Suffolk, England (d. 1837)

Charles Henry Poor, U.S. Navy Officer, born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1882)
Julia Margaret Cameron, English photographer, born in Calcutta, India (d. 1879)
Robert Huston Milroy, American Major General (Union Army), born in Salem, Indiana (d. 1890)
Samuel Davis Sturgis, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Shippensburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1889)

James Lawson Kemper, American lawyer, military officer (US Army Captain - Mexican-American War; Confederate Army Major General), and politician (Governor of Virginia, 1874-78), born in Madison County, Virginia (d. 1895)

Hendrik Gerhard, Dutch tailor, workers' rights activist (founder - Dutch Chapter of International Workingmen's Association), and "father of socialism in the Netherlands" (Chairman of Dutch Social-Democratic (SDB) political party), born in Delft, Netherlands (d. 1886)
Henri Braekeleer, Flemish painter, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1888)
Carl von Linde, German engineer and chemist who invented mechanical refrigeration, born in Berndorf, Germany (d. 1934)
Prince Alfred, Liechtenstein noble and politician, born in Prague, Czech Republic (d. 1907)
William Louis Marshall, American general and engineer, born in Washington, Kentucky (d. 1920)
Millicent Fawcett, English leader of women's rights, born in London (d. 1929)
Joseph Vézina, Québécois conductor, composer, organist, and music professor, born in Quebec City, British Province of Canada (d. 1924)
Sigismund Vladislavovich Zaremba, Ukrainian-Russian composer, born in Zhytomyr, Ukraine (d. 1915)
Eduard David, German politician (Constitution of Weimar), born in Ediger-Eller, Germany (d. 1930)
Jean de Merode, Belgian lieutenant colonel, Grand Marshal of the Court of Belgium, and Prince de Merode, born in Paris, France (d. 1933)
J. H. Leopold, Dutch poet (translated Omar Khayyam), born in 's-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands (d. 1925)
Charles Fabry, French physicist, discoverer of ozone layer in upper atmosphere, born in Marseille, France (d. 1945)
Richard Stöhr, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1967)
Alfred L. Kroeber, American anthropologist and author (Anthropologist looks at History), born in Hoboken, New Jersey (d. 1960)
Renee Vivien, English-born poet, wrote in French, born in London (d. 1909)

Roger Bresnahan, American Baseball Hall of Fame catcher, outfielder and manager (World Series 1905 NY Giants; St. Louis Cardinals; first shin guards and batting helmet), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 1944)
David Barnard Steinman, American civil engineer and bridge designer (Hudson, Triborough), born in New York City (d. 1960)
Lillian Fontaine, British actress (Suddenly it's Spring), born in Reading, Berkshire, England (d. 1975)
Bartolomeo Vanzetti, Italian anarchist (Sacco and Vanzetti Trial), born in Villafalletto, Italy (d. 1927)

Dai Vernon [David Frederick Wingfield Verner], Canadian magician, The Professor, born in Ottawa, Ontario (d. 1992)
